original_hypothesis.title: De-whitelisting USDC Version Permanently Locks Deposited Funds
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If the owner de-whitelists a USDC version via updateWhitelist(_version, false) while usdcBalances[_version] > 0, depositors of that version can no longer withdraw. The withdraw function requires whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion] == true. asdUSDC tokens remain burnable but the underlying USDC is permanently locked. There is no migration path or emergency withdrawal mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a dead-end state: tokens deposited under a now-removed whitelist entry have no withdrawal path. The state transition from whitelisted to de-whitelisted is irreversible for existing deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion], ...) in withdraw; no check in updateWhitelist that usdcBalances == 0 before de-whitelisting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Whitelist removal has no guard against non-zero balances

operational_questions: What operational procedures exist for USDC version migration?
Can the owner re-whitelist to allow withdrawals?
validation_requirements: De-whitelist version with deposits, attempt withdrawal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposits made under whitelisted USDC version
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner de-whitelists the version (routine migration or operational error)

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can re-whitelist to restore access, but if version is compromised, this creates a security dilemma

validation_result.mechanism: missing guard on whitelist removal with outstanding balances
validation_result.impact_summary: De-whitelisting a USDC version with non-zero usdcBalances blocks all withdrawals for that version. Funds are locked until owner re-whitelists, with no emergency withdrawal path.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a code defect in the interaction between `updateWhitelist` and `withdraw`. The `updateWhitelist` function at asdUSDC.sol sets `whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion] = _isWhitelisted` with no check on `usdcBalances[_usdcVersion]`. The `withdraw` function at asdUSDC.sol line 50 requires `whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]` to be true. This creates a dead-end state: deposits exist but cannot be withdrawn. The `recover` function cannot help because it only recovers the difference between actual balance and tracked balance (i.e., accidentally sent tokens), not tracked deposits. The owner CAN re-whitelist to restore access, so 'permanently locked' is overstated. However, the code defect is real: there is no validation preventing an inconsistent state where user funds are inaccessible. If the owner renounces ownership, loses keys, or if the USDC version must remain de-whitelisted for security reasons (e.g., compromised bridge token), the funds become truly permanently locked with no alternative withdrawal mechanism.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies lines 50-52 of asdUSDC.sol as the whitelist check in withdraw. The updateWhitelist function (lines 28-30) has no guard checking usdcBalances. Both code paths are confirmed in the source.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check that usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] == 0 before allowing de-whitelisting. This allows the owner to create a state where deposited funds are inaccessible via withdraw.
The whitelist check on line 51 blocks all withdrawals for de-whitelisted USDC versions, even when usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] > 0 and real USDC tokens are held by the contract.

calling_sequence: 1. Users deposit USDC-v1 via deposit() -> usdcBalances[USDC-v1] > 0, asdUSDC minted
2. Owner calls updateWhitelist(USDC-v1, false) -> whitelistedUSDCVersions[USDC-v1] = false
3. User calls withdraw(USDC-v1, amount) -> reverts at require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]) on line 51
4. No alternative withdrawal path exists - recover() only handles untracked balance surplus
5. USDC-v1 tokens remain locked in the contract
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: updateWhitelist sets whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion] = false (line 29) with no check on usdcBalances[_usdcVersion]. Subsequently, withdraw checks require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]) at line 51 and reverts. The recover function (lines 68-76) computes amountToRecover = balanceOf - usdcBalances, which is 0 when all tokens are properly tracked, so it cannot help. deposit() also checks the whitelist (line 40), so no new deposits can be made either. The only recovery is owner calling updateWhitelist(version, true) again.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script deployASDRouter.js shows updateWhitelist being called with a single address. No operational procedures for version migration are evident.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario is valid: (1) Users deposit whitelisted USDC-v1, getting asdUSDC. (2) Owner de-whitelists USDC-v1 via updateWhitelist(USDC-v1, false). (3) Users attempting withdraw(USDC-v1, amount) get revert at line 51. (4) No emergency withdrawal exists. The owner can re-whitelist to restore access, but this is not guaranteed and creates a security dilemma if the de-whitelisting was done because USDC-v1 is compromised. The defect is the missing guard in updateWhitelist, not the existence of the whitelist check in withdraw.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium-to-High impact. User funds become temporarily inaccessible upon de-whitelisting. If owner cannot or will not re-whitelist (key loss, renounced ownership, security concern with the USDC version), funds are permanently locked. The asdUSDC tokens remain in circulation but have no withdrawal path for that specific USDC version, breaking the backing invariant for those deposits. Users holding asdUSDC could still withdraw other whitelisted USDC versions if those have sufficient balance, but this drains other depositors' reserves.
recommended_mitigations: Add a require(usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] == 0, 'Outstanding deposits') check in updateWhitelist when _isWhitelisted is false
Add an emergency withdrawal function that allows users to withdraw from de-whitelisted versions (possibly with a timelock or governance approval)
Implement a migration function that allows the owner to convert balances from one USDC version to another when de-whitelisting
Consider adding a timelock to whitelist changes to give users time to withdraw before de-whitelisting takes effect
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ASDUSDC. 2. Whitelist a mock USDC token. 3. Deposit some amount from user A. 4. Verify usdcBalances[mockUSDC] > 0. 5. Owner calls updateWhitelist(mockUSDC, false). 6. User A calls withdraw(mockUSDC, amount) and verify it reverts with 'USDC version not whitelisted'. 7. Verify recover() returns 0 (no untracked balance). 8. Verify owner can call updateWhitelist(mockUSDC, true) to restore access. 9. Test edge case where owner renounces ownership after de-whitelisting to confirm permanent lock.
uncertainties: The hypothesis states 'permanently locks' but the owner can re-whitelist. The permanent lock only occurs if ownership is renounced or keys are lost.
It is unclear whether operational procedures exist outside the code to prevent this scenario (e.g., multi-sig governance, documented runbooks).
If users hold asdUSDC, they could potentially withdraw a different whitelisted USDC version (if balance exists), partially mitigating the lock.
additional_testing_needed: Test cross-version withdrawal: can a user who deposited USDC-v1 withdraw USDC-v2 if it has sufficient balance?
Test that recover() truly cannot extract tracked funds for de-whitelisted versions
Test ownership renunciation followed by de-whitelist to confirm permanent lock scenario
